source: Rocky Linux Admin Guide, ch. 4.6 Search (pp. 57-60)

questions.q: What is the difference in purpose between find and grep?
options: find searches for files/directories; grep searches for a string inside a file
find searches inside files; grep searches for files
Both search file contents; find is just faster
Both list files; grep only works on directories
questions.why: find locates files and directories; grep searches for a string within a file's contents.

questions.q: Search under /home for files whose name matches test* (starting from /home).
questions.why: find searches the given directory tree; -name matches by file name. If no directory is given it searches from the current directory.

questions.q: In 'find /tmp -name "*.txt" -exec rm -f {} \;', what do {} and \; mean?
options: {} is replaced by each result; \; ends the -exec command (the ; is escaped so the shell does not grab it)
{} is an empty set; \; starts a comment
{} groups the options; \; is a line continuation
{} is the current directory; \; runs the command in the background
questions.why: find substitutes each matching path for {} and runs the command once per result; the ; terminates -exec and must be escaped as \; so the shell does not interpret it first.

questions.q: Using find's own delete action, remove every *.txt file under /tmp.
questions.why: -delete does the same job as '-exec rm -f {} \;' but is built into find.

questions.q: Find only the BINARY file associated with the command ls.
questions.why: whereis locates files related to a command; -b restricts it to the binary (-m for man pages, -s for source).

questions.q: Search /etc/passwd for lines containing the exact word 'root:'.
questions.why: grep returns the whole line containing the string; -w would restrict the match to a whole word.

questions.q: In grep, what do the -i and -v options do?
options: -i ignores case; -v excludes (inverts) lines containing the string
-i includes case; -v shows only matching words
questions.why: -i ignores case, -v shows the lines that do NOT contain the string, -w matches whole words.

questions.q: List the lines of /etc/passwd that START with 'root' (anchored to the beginning of the line).
questions.why: The ^ anchor matches the start of a line; $ would anchor to the end.

questions.q: Among the SHELL wildcards, what do *, ? and [ ] match?
options: * any string (or none), ? exactly one character, [ ] one character from the listed set
* one character, ? any string, [ ] a range only
* a digit, ? a letter, [ ] any file
All three match exactly one character
questions.why: * stands for any string including empty, ? for a single character, [123] for one character among those listed. Quote wildcards so the shell does not expand them, and don't confuse them with grep's regular-expression meta-characters.
